items.title: Final Fantasy XIV Online Complete Edition - Square Enix bets on subscription depth
items.summary: Final Fantasy XIV Online Complete Edition bundles the base game and all major expansions into one su
items.content:
Final Fantasy XIV Online Complete Edition bundles the base game and all major expansions into one subscription-ready MMO package. This product is driving the price of Square Enix Holdings stock (ISIN JP3967200001).

Square Enix Holdings (siehe oben), JP3967200001, Illustration mit AI erstellt. /* Modal-Breite mobil begrenzen */ @media (max-width: 768px) { .pp_pic_holder { width: 95% !important; left: 2.5% !important; } .pp_content { width: 100% !important; height: auto !important; } .pp_details { width: 100% !important; } .pp_fade { width: 100% !important; } img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; } } const lightbox = GLightbox({ selector: '.glightbox', touchNavigation: true, loop: true, zoomable: true, draggable: true, openEffect: 'zoom', closeEffect: 'fade', slideEffect: 'slide', plyr: { css: '', js: '', } }); Final Fantasy XIV Online Complete Edition greets players with a glowing Eorzean sky on their monitor, soft ambient music in the background and a character creator full of sliders and colors. You can almost hear Naoki Yoshida explaining why this bundle matters for newcomers.

Square Enix built the Final Fantasy XIV Online Complete Edition as a single purchase that unlocks the base game A Realm Reborn plus the major expansions Heavensward, Stormblood, Shadowbringers and Endwalker for new accounts. The bundle is positioned as the cleanest way to get up to date before the next expansion Dawntrail.

The Complete Edition includes 30 days of access time, after which a recurring subscription is required to keep playing. In practice that means the product serves as a paid onboarding funnel into the long-tail subscription revenue of Final Fantasy XIV Online. For retail investors, that recurring model is more relevant than individual box sales.

How Final Fantasy XIV Online underpins Square Enix Holdings revenue and strategy across regions.

Investor Relations Pricing, platforms and content load On the official Square Enix store, the Windows PC version of Final Fantasy XIV Online Complete Edition is currently listed at around 39.99 USD, while the PlayStation and Xbox digital stores carry similar pricing tiers in their respective regions. With regional taxes and promotions, European prices hover in the 39.99 EUR band.

The bundle is available digitally on Windows PC, PlayStation 4, PlayStation 5 and Xbox Series X|S, while physical copies exist in selected markets mainly for console. Each platform taps into the same data centers and content cadence, managed centrally by producer and director Naoki Yoshida and his development team in Tokyo.

Mechanically, Final Fantasy XIV Online remains a traditional tab-target MMO built around cooperative dungeon runs, raids and story quests that can easily stretch into hundreds of hours for a single character. The Complete Edition unlocks that entire story arc from level 1 through the current level cap, offering a continuous narrative without extra expansion purchases.

From a consumer angle, that sheer content volume is both the strength and the hurdle of the product. Marketing director Toshio Murouchi regularly stresses in livestreams that new players should take their time, not rush, and use the duty finder tools to match with others rather than treating it like a single-player game. For subscription retention, the social layer is crucial.

After the included 30 days, the service moves to monthly fees that vary by region and character slot tier. In Europe, standard subscriptions currently start around 10.99 EUR per month, with entry-tier options slightly below that. North America and Japan have their own price points, but the principle is identical.

Final Fantasy XIV Online also runs an optional item shop, the Mog Station, where players can purchase mounts, costumes and services such as additional retainers or story skips. While the Complete Edition itself does not include these extras, it functions as the foundation that later enables upselling on cosmetics and account services.

Square Enix treats the MMO as an ongoing service with large expansions approximately every two to three years and regular smaller patches in between. Endwalker marked the end of the first overarching saga, and Dawntrail is positioned as the start of a new arc, making the Complete Edition a logical way to get players caught up before crossing into the new continent.

During expansion launch windows, the company often runs promotions on the Complete Edition, temporarily cutting the price or offering extra in-game items to nudge fence sitters. Those marketing beats translate into spikes in active subscriptions and, by extension, predictable revenue surges for Square Enix Holdings.

Technically, Final Fantasy XIV Online operates across multiple physical data centers in Japan, North America and Europe, each with several logical worlds. Recent infrastructure upgrades, including additional data centers and hardware improvements, were rolled out to reduce login congestion and improve server stability during peak times.

items.title: Final Fantasy XIV Euro Fanfest Stage and Stream Schedule Announced, Including More Evercold Details
items.summary: Square Enix has revealed the schedule for livestream and stage events at the upcoming Final Fantasy XIV Fanfest in Berlin, Germany.

items.title: Final Fantasy 14 fan need to mark these dates for major Evercold news and more
items.summary: Square Enix is sure to share more information on the upcoming Evercold expansion
items.content:
By Willa Rowe Published Jul 10, 2026, 2:06 PM EDT FYI Square Enix has released the schedule for the upcoming FF14 event in Berlin

The other Final Fantasy 7 Remake you might not know about is shutting down The Summer Game Fest of fighting games had tons of reveals — here's what we learned New Halo board game announced for the end of 2026 Final Fantasy 14 just revealed what to expect at July's Fan Fest Image: Square Enix Sign in to your Polygon.com account With only a couple of weeks before Final Fantasy 14 's Berlin Fan Fest, Square Enix is finally teasing what the community can expect at the event. The Fan Fest stage and stream schedule has been released , giving fans some insight into what will be announced in Berlin from July 25-26.

Excitement for the MMO's next expansion, Evercold , has been growing since its announcement in April . With a January 2027 release window, the major update is getting closer. Square Enix will take Berlin Fan Fest as an opportunity to share a wealth of new information on the expansion and the numerous changes it will bring to the MMO. Here's exactly what to expect and how to watch all the biggest events.

The big announcements at Berlin Fan Fest will be about Final Fantasy 14: Evercold , the MMO's next major expansion. It wouldn't be surprising for a solid date to materialize in Berlin. The last Fan Fest also kept things pretty slim with regard to Evercold 's story. As we get closer to release, it is likely the team will share more details on what awaits the Warrior of Light in the snowy new region the expansion takes place in.

There are also plenty of gameplay changes coming with Evercold that the community is awaiting more details on. It was announced the MMO would be getting a new season model. Producer and director Naoki Yoshida assured fans this did not mean a season pass, but clarification on how it works would be welcome. Perhaps the biggest update is the newly announced Evolved Mode for combat, which completely changes how jobs play. No specifics on what the new system will look like were provided during the April Fan Fest. Fans have expressed some concern, so expect a more detailed look into Evolved Mode at Berlin.

The typical announcements Square Enix makes for expansions are also safe bets. It is likely that at least one of the two new jobs that Evercold will introduce will be revealed in Berlin. Don't expect both to be shown off, though, as Square Enix will want to keep some announcements for Tokyo Fan Fest later in the year. There is also a chance we will get a better look at the upcoming Neon Genesis Evangelion crossover that will serve as the Alliance Raid series in Evercold.

It won't be all Evercold , though. The description of "Letter from the Producer" on the official Final Fantasy XIV site says it will cover "all things Beastmaster ." That is the latest limited job to join the game, set to be released on Sept. 8 as part of patch 7.56. The development team has already shown off some of the interesting mechanics it is cooking up for the job, and it'll be nice to get a more holistic vision of its place in the MMO.

Image: Square Enix All the main events at Berlin Fan Fest will be made available to the public through streaming. Warriors of Light can tune in by going to the official Final Fantasy 14 YouTube or Twitch channels. All but two events at the convention will be available to watch for free. Both the piano concert on day one and the Primals performance on day two will be paid streams; details on which Square Enix will share at a future date.

Here is the entire schedule for Berlin Fan Fest so you don't miss any of the biggest announcements.

items.title: Wisdom of Nym: So what will Final Fantasy XIV’s cosmetic ability overlays be like?
items.content:
One of the small but almost completely non-elaborated parts of Final Fantasy XIV’s keynote in April was the idea that abilities are going to be getting cosmetic overrides in Evercold . Now, taken in isolation, this idea isn’t unique. There are lots of other games that have similar functionality, and from a purely mechanical standpoint, there’s no difference between a character swinging a sword to activate an ability and a character summoning a fireball to activate an ability. Heck, there’s no reason mechanically why your character can’t suddenly snap into a t-pose while the text “ANIMATION NOT CREATED” flashes on the screen before a damage number pops up.

I was joking, but now I kind of want that to be an option.

Where was I? Oh, right. The point is that I want to talk a little bit about these cosmetic overrides and how they might look, since right now we have very little information (as is the case for much of the expansion, to be clear) and there’s room to speculate. But at least one speculation I’ve seen and even partly indulged in strikes me as almost certainly wrong, and that’s entirely based on the fact that the one ability we saw was Dragoon’s old pole-dance animation.

Now, let’s be clear, we did indeed see that animation. It was the image that was used to advertise the idea of cosmetic overrides. And that leads to a pretty natural conclusion: that the new cosmetics will be per-ability overrides that replace current animations with older animations that have been removed from the game otherwise. It’s a reasonable assumption that tracks with what we are shown, except for the part where it’s obviously wrong, starting with an obvious example: Viper.

As it stands right now, Viper does not have any animations that have been removed from the game. I don’t see the changes to Evolved Mode resulting in a huge difference in that regard. But even if it does, the simple reality is that Viper doesn’t have a whole lot of animations that it can swap in for existing abilities.

You can extend it a bit by bringing in animations from other jobs that would work reasonably well for Viper, but that’s also a pretty short list. And that’s not even counting the fact that some abilities aren’t even removed from the game, they’re just replaced at higher levels. What do you do with those animations? Do you bring them back as an option?

My gut answer is “yes,” but the point is that no matter how you slice it (that’s a pun, yes), there are just going to be fewer animations in the “removed from game but now restored” category than you need to make this a meaningful vector of rewards. But I also find myself thinking that if these cosmetics are applied on an ability-by-ability basis, that’s also not a viable option for two reasons.

The first reason is that Yoshida loves to explain the volume of database information each character in FFXIV contains. It comes up constantly. If you have to track every single ability that players can modify, even if that’s only a half-dozen abilities per job, that’s a massive database increase for not much net benefit. It’s obviously something a game can do , but I don’t see it getting welded into this game.

Second reason? Evolved mode. You have a lot more “smart” abilities in Evolved Mode and a general pruning of some options, and that’s going to make the whole thing feel less appealing from that side. We don’t know which abilities are going to be retained and which ones aren’t, but as a general rule of thumb, it’s not going to get people excited to replace an ability you only see half the time if that often.

“All right then, genius, what do you think it’s actually going to look like?” Well, I think there’s actually a template to look toward, and that’s Summoner. I’m going to be completely frank: This sort of upgrade is basically a backdoor to finally getting options other than Titan, Garuda, and Ifrit, and if we don’t get those options when we finally get cosmetic changes, then what are we even doing here? It’s functionally built in.

The thing is that Titan, Garuda, and Ifrit are not actually individual abilities for Summoner. They are , but they’re actually a small handful of abilities. But if you conceive of these abilities like that – as being actually a small handful of abilities, with Summoner functionally divided into a handful of handfuls, suddenly the path for cosmetic overrides makes a lot more sense. Summoner doesn’t have one ability to customize, they have a few different summons to customize which inform other abilities.

This extends naturally to other jobs. Black Mage is really its damage cycle, its restore MP cycle, and maybe a few other assorted abilities. Paladin has its spells, its swordplay, and so on. You can pretty easily narrow down a small set of abilities to adjust, like making Monk more decidedly about kicks or more about arm strikes, and then move forward based on that for a narrow customization “suite,” so to speak.

If I sound a bit excited about this, it’s in no small part because this is exactly the sort of thing I had hoped would come to abilities for years now. One of the things that I’ve noted for a long time is that there’s a definite situation where you have multiple potential flavors for jobs that don’t play differently, like a version of Black Mage that just uses different elements, and this would provide that sort of opportunity in a deft fashion. So I’m excited about the prospect that the developers not only see things in a similar light but are actively leaning into it.

That having been said, this is all speculation. I do not, at this point, know anything more than you do. It’s entirely possible that the field is going to be far more narrow than what I’ve speculated about, or even that the feature gets completely discarded as being unworkable well before release. (It is technically not listed on the Evercold site, after all.) But I suspect this is what we’ve been teased at this point, and while I’ll be happy with anything we get, this is the sort of thing that I do get excited about.

items.title: Final Fantasy XIV - Dancing Mad Ultimate Impressions
items.summary: James checked out the latest raid to hit FFXIV Dawntrail, Dancing Mad (Ultimate), seeing how it stacks up as we head towards the next expansion for the MMORPG.
items.content:
James Galizio Posted: Jul 1, 2026 2:30 PM ET Category: Editorials The Eorzea Prospect 0

On June 2, 2026, Final Fantasy XIV: Dawntrail's second and final Ultimate raid, Dancing Mad (Ultimate), launched for the game, and besides some jank, which was just hotfixed last week, it's fair to say that the community reception has been positive - especially with consideration to Future Rewritten (Ultimate), and its own reception.

If you aren't an Ultimate raider, there are a few things to note about Futures Rewritten. First off, the game's two previous Ultimate raids from Endwalker , Dragonsong Reprise (Ultimate) and The Omega Protocol (Ultimate), were both notable steps up in terms of mechanical complexity and overall difficulty at their release. The latter, perhaps, to an overdone degree. The one-two-three punch of DSR, to Abyssos, to TOP was enough to burn many raiders out of the game, and I can testify that was very much the case for many raiders I've known firsthand.

FRU, then, was an easier encounter. With one or two exceptions, mechanics were easier. Phase 2, in particular, might as well be a joke by Ultimate standards. Mitigation and healing checks, similarly, weren't very tough - the roughest tankbusters in the fight were, arguably, in the first phase. The fight was fun, and I would never say it was an easy fight - it wasn't, and isn't - but it's very fair to say that at least some raiders felt it was an overcorrection.

The biggest issue with the fight was, and remains, downtime. Downtime mechanics were a big reason why Pictomancer was such a powerhouse, as players could prep their bursts for once the boss became targetable again to a degree that no other class was capable of. The idea of downtime mechanics is ideally to make mechanics difficult enough that players wouldn't be able to focus on both doing the mechanic while dealing damage, and yet that wasn't exactly how things turned out for FRU.

Mad (Ultimate) is different. There is no downtime, and the mechanics aren't just a tad tougher than in FRU; they're more punishing. Chances are, even if you have no intention of challenging the fight yourself, you've heard about the "fake out" ending. FRU also had a puzzle gating the final phase, but the actual mechanics behind it amounted to "don't stand in one specific, static location on the arena".

Phases 1 and 2 are far more engaging than their counterparts in FRU. Phases 3 and 4 are far more challenging. Healing and mitigation checks are more technically demanding, not just in terms of the challenge of keeping a team topped up, but with mechanics that task players with carefully healing up players at a staggered pace - all the while doing mechanics. Dancing Mad is a very tough fight, tougher than its contemporary, but the difficulty is managed in a far healthier manner.

You could argue that the fight lacks spectacle, but frankly - who cares? Not every Ultimate raid needs to be the same thing, which is where this one excels - and exactly why it feels like the perfect cherry on top for an expansion that, if nothing else, has been excellent for raiders whenever it took the chance to think outside the box. With Final Fantasy XIV's future looking so promising, it feels great to bow out of Dawntrail on such a high note.

In this weekly column, Jatobi takes a look at the world of FFXIV and the many things going on in the endless adventure.

James Galizio has been writing about gaming and technology from LA since 2014, with bylines at outlets such as Game Informer, Kotaku and RPG Site. He plays way too much Final Fantasy XIV, but you can also usually find him playing the latest RPG out of Japan. He loves to spoil his cat, Mishy.

items.title: Choose My Adventure: Steamrolling low-level bosses and storming roulettes in Final Fantasy XIV
items.content:
It had been a long and tiring day, which only made the bath feel that much better for her. She let out a soft sigh of relief as she felt the ache and the sweat of her rigors slowly ebb away from her body with the gentle rippling current of her bathtub. For once, her usual soak felt earned instead of routine.

As she let the warm water idly massage her, she thought back to all of the rigor that she put herself through. Small but meaningful victories that all added up to the slaying of apathy. At least this time around; the trick would be to do it again.

After she dried herself off and put on her favorite silken slip, she curled up into a little nook tucked in the corner of her house lined with pillows, soft plushes, and books to continue reading one of her favorite novels. Another part of her pre-sleep ritual, yet it once more felt earned. She would definitely keep this up tomorrow. And onward.

As I mentioned at the start of this slate of columns, most of the reason I kept my subscription to Final Fantasy XIV active was to keep my in-game house, having mostly let myself sink into a sort of self-defeating quagmire about the game, my playstyle, and my ability. But now I feel pretty recharged.

What helped was, of course, absolutely murdering the hell out of the Ex versions of boss fights. I had a suspicion that things would be pretty simple, but I didn’t know that, at least from the start, it would be that simple.

I once again was able to enlist the assistance of my husband and a couple of other friends, one of whom is very much the sort of endgame player who likes to stay on the higher end of content. They don’t go for world first clears, but they also don’t get left behind too far, and they certainly don’t shy away from the challenge, so they were the perfect person to get me started.

But then when we started running into the “harder” versions of the game’s base eikons, they were falling over with literally a single attack. It was jarring and hilarious in the best way possible — a jolt to the system that immediately disabused me about how tough this sort of thing could be. I knew we were basically 50 levels over their heads, but it was still surprising.

This, of course, meant that not only could I clear off things from a long and dusty to-do list, but I could also farm for mounts. That seems like such a simple thing, but having missed out on the opportunity for so long, I took heart in being able to clear that task. It’s honestly kind of hard to put into words.

My friend entourage and I continued the walk up through A Realm Reborn and some of Heavensward, all the while mostly taking the foes down in just a few hits. While the actual mechanical challenge wasn’t really there, I still did make sure to try to read the tells and position accordingly as if I did need to focus appropriately. It was soft training, sure, but it did once again reaffirm to me that I could mostly follow along with what was happening.

By the time we were taking on a couple of Stormblood’s foes, I was actively interested in seeing some actually harder hitting EX fights, which brought my guide to discuss how they first got started doing these battles at current item level. It really was just a matter of plugging their nose and diving in, and while they did have some bumpy spots, they ultimately were able to find people and get wins.

I appreciate that doing unsynced EX fights is most definitely not a 1:1 comparison to current content. Obviously . This was less about knowing what the current bleeding edge was like and more about reassurance and a little bit of confidence building, not just through camaraderie but also through my own active participation. I am genuinely emboldened to at least try to see what the sterner boss battles are like. Especially with the right company.

This self-empowerment carried over when I started to do the roulette content to power up my Gunbreaker, as I took the lead both in terms of party role and engaging the random runs. Again, a meager step in the grand scheme, I know, but for someone whose confidence was jarred and who let himself believe his doubts as ironclad fact, this meant a lot.

I suppose that’s really the story about this series: the exercise I wanted to perform. I mean, I was self-indulgent enough to write things in-character for this website, which isn’t something I’ve done on any sort of really public platform before now. I needed to just… push. Push past. Push through. Thanks to those who did read all of those, by the way. I don’t believe I’m some Tolkien-level novelist, but being able to do this meant a lot. Doing all of this meant a lot.

Of course, that’s basically leading into my own personal progress and away from the focus of this column as we slide into July. And for this month, I feel like I have almost no choice but to play one game alongside all of you: Destiny 2 .

items.title: Wisdom of Nym: Why do so many of Final Fantasy XIV’s base jobs struggle with identity?
items.content:
As Evercold approaches for Final Fantasy XIV , I hope that everyone is ready to have a new version of Monk, Dragoon, Bard, and Summoner. At the very least. When I speculated on the jobs I expect to be the most different in Evolved mode, those were all in the crosshairs, and they’re not really alone. Three of those jobs are pretty explicitly the “we’re still trying to figure out the job while the game is in motion” poster children. I would be more surprised if an expansion didn’t have a big revision for Monk at this point.

This is interesting to me because we’re also talking about the most core jobs to the game as a whole. The jobs that were available in the game’s initial relaunch have seemingly gone through the largest number of revisions, and you might say, “Well, they’ve been around the longest,” but that’s in contrast to most other jobs. Red Mage and Samurai haven’t ever had a real fundamental redesign to the extent of the older jobs, and Stormblood was back in 2017. And what makes it even more compelling to me is that the usual explanation that would track is not true here: This is not a different team.

For better or for worse, it’s not exactly new to observe that when a new team comes on to an MMORPG, they’re likely to redesign parts of the game to be closer to their vision. Considering that Final Fantasy XIV’s relaunch is now more than a decade in the rearview, I think it would make sense if there were people who came to the game with a preference for older versions of the game. You can really feel the influence of the new design leads and game director.

Except that’s not actually true, which is why I made the joke about the new director. The game doesn’t have a new director; it has the same director, who has been the director for the game’s entire relaunch now. That’s what makes it particularly interesting. This isn’t a matter of new staff coming in with different ideas but the same people changing their minds about what they want from these jobs time and again.

Why is that? You’d expect things like Monk and Dragoon to be some of the most fundamental components of the game’s identity. And the thing is that I think they kind of are, but they’re also victims of the problem of how to manifest the entire franchise.

Let’s take a step back and look at Monk. I mean, really, let’s look at Monk through the ages because Monk is one of the most quintessentially Final Fantasy jobs possible. It has been in the game since the first installment. Every game with a job system has it. Almost every single game that doesn’t have jobs has someone who is clearly a Monk (Josef, Yang, Sabin, Tifa, Zell, Amarant, Snow), and the ones where there isn’t a clear Monk in your party still have someone with clear parallels or who just require the smallest bit of squinting. Monk is always a thing.

So what makes a Monk unique? What unifies all of these characters? Therein lies something of a problem because the answer is… actually kinda vague beyond “they all punch stuff.” Monk’s utility and worth varies a lot between games.

FFXIV has always had Pugilist and then Monk, and the idea of “punching” has been part of the game since the beginning. Yet at the same time, it has a lot of ground to cover there which other jobs don’t, and it is a more amorphous concept just from the word go. The early concept for Monk at launch was very much about a job that had something of a perpetual combo going, weaving between its weapon skills in and out of stances instead of along a specific set of combos.

The problem, of course, was that this meant Monk suffered a lot during periods when the boss was impossible to target. So skills were added to solve that problem, but also that caused other issues with Monk rotations. Monk has always been assembled from individual parts that make sense and feel Monk-like, but Monk as an idea in this franchise has a lot of plasticity.

By contrast, Red Mage has a clearer focus here. Red Mage, since its creation, has been a job about balancing spellcasting and building to a melee combo. That is what Red Mage is . It was designed, first and foremost, to fit into a specific design and function according to that, and any changes are gauged against how well they balance against that basic design and function.

Lots of the things Monk was designed to do in ARR have slowly become not a vital component of the game over time. Dragoon having increasingly long combos became a problem as any slip meant that Dragoon had to start from zero all over again. Summoner, as a concept, has gone through three different variants of “how do we summon Bahamut” because that is traditionally the iconic Big Summon of the franchise. But it’s hard to keep adding new stuff when you’ve already added Bahamut to the game.

Last but not least, a lot of the changes have also come into play as new jobs have entered the mix and changed the game. Reaper, for example, is in many ways modeled after Dark Knight from Final Fantasy XI , specifically using a scythe instead of a greatsword. I mentioned how Viper was able to take a lot of the “Rogue as damage-dealer” ideas that no longer fit in Ninja, leaving Ninja to be more focused on Ninja Stuff. As these new jobs create a new focus, old jobs can adopt different identities in turn, even if that changes the identity of what those jobs used to be.

Oh, and let’s not forget that the initial job designs were also built around the idea that a single class would become multiple different jobs, something that did not work. Classes being completely deprecated is one of those elements that the designers just were not building for in 2013.

In summary, there are a lot of factors that have led to these jobs just steadily changing more and more over time, until their present forms are largely new identities from the original. Paladin cares about MP in a way it never did in the base game. Monk currently plays more like a support version of Viper than anything. These things have changed, in many cases significantly.

items.title: Final Fantasy 14 devs looked to the MMO's best expansion to design the Arcadion series after feeling like "ensuring player comfort and stress reduction" limited creativity
items.summary: Heavensward, beloved by fans and Square Enix alike
items.content:
Heavensward, beloved by fans and Square Enix alike

When you purchase through links on our site, we may earn an affiliate commission. Here’s how it works .

(Image credit: Square Enix) Copy link Facebook X Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 2 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter With Final Fantasy 14 expansion Evercold underway, it's hard for us raiders not to consider what the 8.0 tier might look like – after all, Dawntrail's own Arcadion arguably stands as one of the best the MMO has seen to date.

Just why is it so good, though? Dawntrail as a whole left many players disappointed, after all, teetering back and forth from "Mostly Negative" reviews on Steam since its release – but its raid series, the Arcadion, never met that sort of poor reception itself. I got to ask assistant director Tsuyoshi Yokozawa about this, questioning what Square Enix had learned from its past tiers… and how it implemented this knowledge into the Arcadion.

"Our experience from previous raid series, along with feedback from players, played a very important role in the design philosophy of the Arcadion," he tells me. "In previous series, especially at the time of Pandæmonium, our emphasis was on a policy of comprehensively ensuring player comfort and stress reduction. This approach did help make content more accessible," but it also limited developers' ability to "explore more creative ideas."

They saw another problem there, too. As Yokozawa says, "There was less room for individual players to demonstrate their true abilities, resulting in a heightened sensation of 'going through the motions' when tackling the fight. To break through these challenges, we decided that with the Arcadion, we would return to the mindset we had during the Heavensward era." That's right – the Alexander era. I love it, you probably love it, many fans love it. I've even got a soft spot in my heart for the more recent Ultimate.

If you're newer to the MMO, you might be wondering what he means here, however. He explains: "This entailed not being bound by convenience alone, but rather focused first and foremost on 'novel experiences and surprises,' approaching encounter design with a freer, more flexible perspective." Square Enix also "placed importance on striking a balance, where overcoming moderate challenges would deliver a strong sense of achievement."

Final Fantasy 14 Evercold raids follow The Arcadion's lead as assistant director teases "novelty" How Final Fantasy 14's best Dawntrail raids serve as "a firm guideline for our future battle content development" As Final Fantasy 14 looks to the future, I hope Square Enix remembers the quest that got me excited for Dawntrail That's why the lead thinks the team was "able to create exciting battles filled with new surprises" out of the Arcadion – "and that led to positive feedback from many players." It certainly did. I, for one, see it as one of the best Savage (and Normal, honestly) tiers. The Arcadion is up there with the Alexander raids in terms of novelty, coming as a much-needed breath of fresh air amid Dawntrail's other content.

Yokozawa stresses this once more, describing the Arcadion as "a return to the design philosophy that prioritized 'surprise and novelty,' which we pursued at the time of Heavensward." He concludes that, "By building mechanics with a more open, unrestricted mindset, we feel that this approach resonated well with the expectations of today's players." I'd say he's most definitely not wrong here.

Weekly digests, tales from the communities you love, and more

Across discussions on forums online, you'll see people praising the Arcadion as a top-tier… well, tier. Heck, you'll even find folks noting that it's as boundary-pushing as Heavensward's raid series was. I'm just hopeful Evercold's will prove similarly brilliant.

Final Fantasy 14 Evercold raids follow The Arcadion's lead as the MMO's assistant director teases they'll value "surprise and novelty" over "convenience and efficiency."

After spending years with her head in various fantastical realms' clouds, Anna studied English Literature and then Medieval History at the University of Edinburgh, going on to specialize in narrative design and video game journalism as a writer. She has written for various publications since her postgraduate studies, including Dexerto, Fanbyte, GameSpot, IGN, PCGamesN, and more. When she's not frantically trying to form words into coherent sentences, she's probably daydreaming about becoming a fairy druid and befriending every animal or she's spending a thousand (more) hours traversing the Underdark in Baldur's Gate 3. If you spot her away from her PC, you'll always find Anna with a fantasy book, a handheld video game console of some sort, and a Tamagotchi or two on hand. She has written for various publications since her postgraduate studies, including Dexerto, Fanbyte, GameSpot, IGN, PCGamesN, and more. When she's not frantically trying to form words into coherent sentences, she's probably daydreaming about becoming a fairy druid and befriending every animal or she's spending a thousand (more) hours traversing the Underdark in Baldur's Gate 3. If you spot her away from her PC, you'll always find Anna with a fantasy book, a handheld video game console of some sort, and a Tamagotchi or two on hand.

items.content:
The collaboration event with Dragon Quest X, Breaking Brick Mountains, returns Thursday, June 25! Battle against gargantuan golems from the Dragon Quest series, once again making their way to Eorzea! Don't miss this opportunity to earn rich rewards, including the king slime crown, thug's mug, and the newly added Dragon Quest X framer's kit. * This event is identical to the one previously held. * If you have already completed the event, you can replay the quests via the seasonal event replay feature. For details, please check the special site.

Event Duration

Thursday, June 25, 2026 at 1:00 a.m. (PDT) / 8:00 (GMT) / 9:00 (BST) / 18:00 (AEST) to Monday, July 13, 2026 at 7:59 a.m. (PDT) / 14:59 (GMT) / 15:59 (BST) /14 July at 0:59 (AEST)

How to Participate

Begin the quest "Breaking Brick Mountains" by speaking to Havak Alvak of the Mythril Eye in Ul'dah – Steps of Nald (X:12.1 Y:8.2). During this event, players can purchase a King Slime Crown by speaking with the Toughie in Ul'dah – Steps of Nald (X:8.2 Y:12.2).

Rewards

Visit the special site for more details.

items.title: FINAL FANTASY XIV: DAWNTRAIL – THE ART OF SUCCESSION -RELICS OF ETERNITY- ANNOUNCED
items.summary: English-Language Edition of Art Book FINAL FANTASY XIV: Dawntrail – The Art of Succession -Relics of Eternity- Available December 2026
items.content: LOS ANGELES (June 22, 2026) – SQUARE ENIX® recently announced that the English-language publication for volume two of the official art book set for FINAL FANTASY XIV: Dawntrail is set to release on December 15, 2026. Following the release of The Art of Succession -Relics of Heritage-, the upcoming volume, -Relics of Eternity- , features hundreds of pages of full-color art and an exclusive in-game item code for a Lolly Cat minion.

items.title: Final Fantasy 14 Rewards Japanese Players for Paying Their Real-Life Taxes With a Giant Dog
items.content: Final Fantasy 14 players in Japan now have the opportunity to get in-game cosmetics and items by paying their real life taxes, as reported by Famitsu . These rewards include the online MMORPG’s Megashiba Mount. More like this Final Fantasy 14's Yoshi-P Weighs in on Mods After Popular Custom Character Sharing Mod Shuts Down Japan has a Hometown tax initiative (Furusato Nozei), which allows people to redirect portions of their residence tax to other areas of the country, even if they don’t live there. In return, you can choose a special gift from the region (everything from local snacks and traditional handcrafted items, to hotel stays and local cultural experiences). The scheme launched back in 2008 with the aim of directing tax funds back to rural, less-populated areas, but now almost every municipality in the country offers some kind of reward via the scheme. It’s a win-win as local governments receive more funds, while taxpayers receive presents in exchange for tax money they would have had to fork out anyway.

items.title: Final Fantasy XIV Items Now Offered As Tax Rewards
items.summary: Shibuya is offering Final Fantasy XIV items as a reward when donating to it via the hometown tax system in Japan.
items.content:
People who donate to Shibuya through the hometown tax system can now receive in-game rewards in Final Fantasy XIV . There are six different rewards that you can get, and what you receive is based on how much money you donate.

To receive your hometown tax donation reward, you can use this website . First, you choose which item you want (and how much you want to donate), and then you’ll go to the item page for it. Once you purchase it, you can get the serial code to redeem your reward in-game.

Here are the rewards and how much money you’ll need to give to receive them:

For reference, the hometown tax system in Japan lets taxpayers in urban cities ‌contribute to rural areas for a tax credit. It was created in response to people who leave their hometowns to work or study in the city, thus paying taxes there and giving nothing back to their hometowns, even though they had used public services there while growing up. To entice people to donate money, many cities offer gifts like food. As a note, some other popular perks from Shibuya include dog pee mats (really), a food processor, and cooking pot.

Final Fantasy XIV is readily available on the PS4, PS5, Xbox Series X, and Windows PC. The Switch 2 version will come out in August 2026.

items.title: Sarah’s Final Fantasy XIV Fan Fest Experience
items.summary: What is the most magical event full of sparkle and wonder that only takes place every couple of years? Why, it's FFXIV FanFest! Sarah Ferries provides a journal of her experience at the event, including the Jollibee and Requiem Café visits.
items.content:
Let’s just start off by saying that the weather was way better in Los Angeles than the previous Fan Fests in Las Vegas. Yes, things like hotels were more expensive, but I would take that any day over the 100+-degree heat. Even Yoshi P (Producer and Director Naoki Yoshida) said he was melting!

I arrived about 8:30am for the 10am keynote to get a good seat. Just walking into the stage area, it felt a lot bigger than in previous years in Vegas. I was in my Pictomancer cosplay, which was a bit chilly, but better than being too hot. It did get warmer as the keynote went on, though. It truly was jam-packed and longer than a normal keynote. I was intending to post updates on social media like normal, but the internet was garbage inside the convention center so I just made notes on my phone, and was at least able to use Discord. That said, I also forgot to get the media-only WiFi information, so that’s partly on me.

Most of the keynote people loved. There was lots of cheering and clapping, especially with the Evercold trailer . However, when it came to showcasing the Reborn/Evolved modes, the people around me didn’t appear to like it, especially after Yoshi P announced that all new jobs will be only using the Evolved mode. I think it was the initial shock of changing things that really set people off, but after talking through it with people, it doesn’t seem like it’s going to be as big of a change as some people think. I was able to meet up with a lot of my friends from Twitch chats and Final Fantasy XIV, and after we all talked about it, I think it will be better. As someone who plays on a controller, this might be of particular benefit to me. I have my own rotation, and I have no idea if that’s the “correct” Pictomancer rotation, but with the evolved mode it might allow for more flexibility.

After the welcome ceremony, I went with a group of friends to Jollibee, which was holding a Final Fantasy XIV collaboration for Fan Fest. Even though the Roulette Game Show looked fun, we were all very hungry. We arrived at Jollibee and it fortunately wasn’t that busy. I think we only waited about a half hour. We wanted to go to this exact one, because we were told this was the only one that was fully decorated. I was amused at G’raha Tia being on the trash cans eating a sandwhich. This seemed to be the best time to go, since outside of Fan Fest people were saying that the lines at other times were many hours long. I got the $21 box to get my fancy emote. I only ate the sandwich there, but it was delicious. I really liked the sauce, and it came with the good reminder that no chocobos were harmed in the making of the food. We were wondering what they were going to do with the decorations after the event was over. I would’ve loved to take one of the aetheryte lights and put it in my room.

We made it back to Fan Fest around 3pm, and I wanted to see some of the developer panel to understand the Evolved mode a little better, but then I got distracted by shiny cosplayers and friends. Unfortunately, by around 4pm my feet hurt so bad I could not walk, which is partly the price I paid for wearing my cosplay shoes, and I ended up going back to my hotel to put on my sneakers before coming back. I was able to get back just in time to watch the “Glamoured to Life” cosplay walk for around twenty minutes before having to go to my meet and greet. I am glad I was able to see some of the streamers I watch walk, including my favorite scene I was able to see: Sultana drinking from the goblet on stage. The crowd reaction was great. She came out and pulled out the cup, at which point you could hear the gasps and the air leave the room. Then she went to drink, to which everyone immediately screamed, and then stood there for a second before giving a thumbs up. That got a lot of cheers.

When I got to my meet and greet, I am surprised how fast and easy it was. At bigger cons, these things are a mess and you often have to wait hours to meet your chosen guest. However, at Fan Fest you simply had to line up fifteen minutes before your appointment, and then go through relatively fast. I was able to meet Jason Charles Miller, who originally voiced Raubahn Aldynn in A Realm Reborn, but is perhaps better known for performing the vocals in the theme songs for Shadowbringers and Endwalker, plus “Unleashed” from Dawntrail . He was very nice and I even got to chat with him a little bit while my photo was taken.

The Piano Performance with Keiko and special guest Amanda Achen was out of this world. As always, Keiko has a beautiful princess dress for the performance. The sound and stage quality was fantastic. There were some camera issues, where the shot stayed on Keiko for a very long time even when Amanda was singing. I’m chalking that up to technical issues, hopefully. I’m so glad I was able to see the stage from my seat so I could see Amanda singing. Luckily, it wasn’t most of the concert, so I was able to watch the big screens on the sides of the stage.

I was able to attend a portion of “From Tacos to Tenders: Serving up the Lyrics of FFXIV ” with Michael-Christopher Koji Fox. The man is hilarious, and I love listening to him talk about the game, the antics, and how he comes up with lyrics. This panel showed how Square Enix really listens to its players as well as showcasing famous misheard lyrics to some of the songs from the game. One of the most misheard lyrics is from Ultima theme, with players chanting along with “big fat tacos” instead of “Beat, the Heart of Sabik.” Typing it out, it seems like a bizarre mishearing, but you’d have to hear it. He then had fans come up on stage to write new lyrics to “eScape”, which the audience voted on. The winning set of lyrics was actually changed for the Primals live concert at the end of the night! VTuber IronMouse was a special guest during this panel and gave a small concert at the end which was actually really good. I had never seen her before, but I liked her performance.

As much as I wanted to see random Q&As with Yoshi P, I hadn’t yet got the chance to play any of the games yet. I heard a lot of them were capping lines at 4:30, so wanted to get as much done as possible, though I am sad I missed the question about the butt slider.

I started with Run Alpaca Run, which is pretty much a carnival race horse game where you roll a ball into various holes: think of a mini version of Skee-Ball. I did not win, but it was still fun. I wasn’t able to get into the Crystalline Conflict Palaistra Challenge, as the line was already too long, and it’s not something I got into in-game, so it’s hard to compare the physical game to the digital area. I did try the Pelupelu Pitch, which is another carnival style game of ring toss. I am also terrible at this game and scored zero points, but it was still fun. Next was Mica’s Magical Maneuvers, which was a reaction-time based game. Sticks drop suddenly and you have to grab them before they hit the floor. Metem’s Limit Break Challenge was another carnival-style game I did not partake in, because of my tiny, sensitive hands. It gives participants one opening and a single punch to score big DPS; I watched a guy punch a score of 649, which was pretty high compared to the others. However, I did try the Arcadion Slam to try out for the Solution Nine Yans; shooting hoops with a basketball that couldn’t go wrong, right? Wrong! In my haste of trying to make baskets in competition with other people on a time limit, the ball bounced off the rim and hit me in the face. Luckily, it didn’t leave a bruise or a bloody nose, just hurt pride and some redness. Last but not least, Ride the Rhythm was the most popular game at Fan Fest. The line was always long, but it was definitely the highlight. This was basically a disco floor that people had to dance on, but with elements from a boss fight, complete with area of effect squares, tankbuster circles, and a stack marker. It was really cool to see boss mechanics from in the game come to life in person. There was one thing I didn’t get to do but I wish I had: the photo spot with G’raha Tia on a gondola. That was probably the second most popular thing at Fan Fest. I did get a photo at Aunt Tii’s Tacos in Tuliyollal, though.

Having Fan Fest modeled after the latest expansion, Dawntrail , and showcasing its main areas like Solution Nine and Tuliyollal was done really well. There were lots of photo opportunities with the giant “XIV” letters, well known landmarks like Aunt Tii’s Tacos, different Solution Nine areas such as a lounge and bar, and even a photo op with Honey B. Lovely, everyone’s favorite boss. (Alright, not everyone’s, but she was pretty popular.) The only thing I would’ve liked more of was an expanded Solution Nine lounge with more couches and chairs, as they were always full of people, meaning many had to sit along the walls or on the floor.

The last hurrah of the evening was a concert from The Primals, the official band of Final Fantasy XIV lead by Sound Director Masayoshi Soken since 2014. The members are Masayoshi Soken, Michael-Christopher Koji Fox, GUNN, Eikichi Iwai, and Tetsuya Tachibana. Last Fan Fest, people brought their own light sticks for the Primals concert and this year Square Enix decided to sell them at their merch station, which made it that much more fun and I was able to get one of the last light sticks before they sold out. There were multiple colors to choose from on the light stick to match the songs the Primals were playing, and everyone in the crowd seemed to be on the same page. The concert began with the “checking member status” display on the video screens, as one would see waiting for a dungeon to fill in game. The band began “Open Sky”, followed by “Scream”, “Ride the Rhythym”, “Not Afraid” (with special guest Yoshi P), “Unleashed”, “Shadowbringers”, “Absolute Tyranny”, “A Long Fall”, “Rise”, “eScape”, and finally “Under the Weight”. I was surprised when the concert ended as I was enjoying it so much I wasn’t ready for it to end.

Before I left Anaheim, I was able to visit the FFXIV Requiem Café. There was not a lot of merch left, and all the pastries and some of the drinks were sold out. I was able to grab a New Adventures drink, which had a sprout bookmark in the whipped cream atop a refreshing mint citrus soda, and the Scree! ice cream drink. This was a brown sugar milk tea with chocolate drizzle topped with a toasted marshmallow cold foam, gold lustre dust, and a serpent gummy. Both of these were VERY delicious. The inside of the café was magical and wondrous, with a fake giant tree that looked like a weeping willow with a few seats inside. It had a TV showing some story quests, as well as many cardboard stand ups of our favorite scions. The store was decked out with gaming computers that had the free trial loaded up for people to play the game, along with a post-it wall for gamers to leave their mark. I wish I could take the tree with me and put it in my house!

Overall this year’s Fan Fest was yards above the Las Vegas one in 2023. The weather was much nicer, there was a bigger space that was laid out much better, and there were better food options. As always, thank you Square Enix for putting on these events!

items.title: FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE is available for pre-purchase now!
items.summary: https://store.steampowered.com/app/3259780/ What if FINAL FANTASY had continued to push the limits of pixel art and evolved from its classic roots? The first FINAL FANTASY to join the HD‑2D lineup, FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE delivers a visuall
items.content:
https://store.steampowered.com/app/3259780/ What if FINAL FANTASY had continued to push the limits of pixel art and evolved from its classic roots? The first FINAL FANTASY to join the HD‑2D lineup, FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE delivers a visually striking experience that feels both nostalgic and new, enhanced by dynamic camera angles and pixelized 3D models that make every moment more dramatic and immersive. Return to the pixel‑art world of FINAL FANTASY, evolved from its classic roots. Travel with companions bound by fate and call on Visions to aid you in battle to uncover the truth behind a force that threatens to bring everything to ruin.

Digital Deluxe Edition:

This product is a bundle that includes the FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE base game along with the Magitek & Grimoire Deluxe Pack, which contains an item that transforms your chocobo's appearance into magitek armor, an item exclusive to this pack that unlocks a certain character's Fel Meteor Limit Burst, and useful items to aid you at the start of your journey. Digital Deluxe Edition Contents: ▼ FINAL FANTASY RESONANCE Base Game ▼ Magitek & Grimoire Deluxe Pack *Images are for illustrative purposes only. Actual in‑game visuals may differ. 　 Pack Contents: 　 • Magitek Armor Key x1 　 • Archwitch’s Grimoire x1 　 • Mastery Ring x1 (Increases MRP gained in battle by a certain amount) 　 • Tent x5 　 • Mist Potion x5 　 • Mist Ether x5 　 • Phoenix Down x5 　 • Remedy x3

Pre-order Bonus: Magitek Airship & Squire Starter Pack

*Images are for illustrative purposes only. Actual in‑game visuals may differ. This set contains an item that transforms your airship’s appearance into a magitek vessel, along with useful items to aid you at the start of your journey. Pack Contents: • Magitek Airship Passkey x1 • Knight’s Greatsword x1 • Chestplate of Preparation x1 (Increases EXP gained in battle by a certain amount) • Magicite Shard x1 • Tent x3 • Mist Potion x3

items.title: Patch 7.51 Notes
items.summary: Patch 7.51 introduces the seventh installment of the ultimate raid series, Dancing Mad (Ultimate), as well as new custom deliveries featuring Tiisol Ja. And for our intrepid Disciples of the Hand and Land, new exploration awaits on Auxesia.
items.content:
Patch 7.51 introduces the seventh installment of the ultimate raid series, Dancing Mad (Ultimate), as well as new custom deliveries featuring Tiisol Ja. And for our intrepid Disciples of the Hand and Land, new exploration awaits on Auxesia. * Additional features will be implemented in future updates:
  • The Occult Crescent: North Horn, Weapon Enhancement Quests - Phantom Weapons, Inconceivably Further Hildibrand Adventures sidequests, and Allied Society capstone sidequests are scheduled to release in Patch 7.55.
  • Further main scenario quests and the beastmaster limited job are scheduled to release in Patch 7.56.
Read the full patch notes on the Lodestone. Launch FINAL FANTASY XIV Now

items.title: The Make It Rain Campaign Begins May 29!
items.summary: The Make It Rain Campaign returns once more to the Gold Saucer! With a 50% MGP bonus and an assortment of adorable otter-themed goods on offer, you won't want to miss out! Furthermore, campaign attendants will be offering a range of items a
items.content:
The Make It Rain Campaign returns once more to the Gold Saucer! With a 50% MGP bonus and an assortment of adorable otter-themed goods on offer, you won't want to miss out! Furthermore, campaign attendants will be offering a range of items at discounted prices, including some exclusive limited-time deals, so be sure to join the festivities and rake in the rewards while you can!

Event Duration

From Friday, May 29, 2026 at 1:00 a.m. (PDT) / 8:00 (GMT) / 9:00 (BST) / 18:00 (AEST) to Wednesday, June 24, 2026 at 7:59 a.m. (PDT) / 14:59 (GMT) / 15:59 (BST) / Thursday, 25 June at 0:59 (AEST)

How to Participate

Begin the quest "You Otter Be There" by speaking to Ollier in Ul’dah - Steps of Nald (X:9.4 Y:9.1). * Players must be level 15 and above to start the quest. * Players must first complete the quest "It Could Happen to You."

Rewards

* MGP rewards from the Gold Saucer have been increased by 50% for the duration of this event. * Certain items available during previous iterations of the event can be obtained via the campaign attendant. * The Crystal Tower striker, Monster Toss machine, Cuff-a-Cur machine, and a number of other prizes may be purchased at a reduced MGP cost from the campaign attendant. Visit the special site for more details.

items.title: Play Free for up to 4 Days in the Latest Free Login Campaign!
items.summary: In the hope that would-be adventurers can experience all that FFXIV has to offer, we are pleased to announce the latest Free Login Campaign. Follow the Trail to the Heavens ! In Patch 7.5, you can face Enuo in the Unmaking, take a stroll th
items.content:
In the hope that would-be adventurers can experience all that FFXIV has to offer, we are pleased to announce the latest Free Login Campaign. Follow the Trail to the Heavens! In Patch 7.5, you can face Enuo in the Unmaking, take a stroll through Windurst: The Third Walk in the FINAL FANTASY XI alliance raid crossover, and leap into battle in Archeia Harmonias, an all-new arena for Crystalline Conflict. A host of other additions and adjustments will make your time in Eorzea more streamlined and enjoyable, including increased furnishing placement limits, the consolidation of dyes, and additional pieces of gear that can now be converted to outfit glamour or stored in the armoire. With the introduction of Patch 7.5, the Shadowbringers expansion can now be played as part of the FINAL FANTASY XIV Starter Edition. The expansion will be automatically registered to your account if you already own the Starter Edition, so why not take this opportunity to jump into the adventure?

If you or any of your friends are currently taking a break from your adventures, you won't want to miss out on this limited-time event! Visit the Free Login Campaign website for details.

items.title: The Maiden's Rhapsody Returns!
items.summary: Experience the intriguing tale bridging Hydaelyn and Vana'diel as The Maiden's Rhapsody─a past collaboration with FFXI─returns to the realm once more! Embark on a journey with one of Vana'diel's heroes and discover what twists of fate broug
items.content:
Experience the intriguing tale bridging Hydaelyn and Vana'diel as The Maiden's Rhapsody─a past collaboration with FFXI─returns to the realm once more! Embark on a journey with one of Vana'diel's heroes and discover what twists of fate brought her to our realm. * This event is identical to the one previously held. * If you have already completed the event, you can replay the quests via the seasonal event replay feature. We highly recommend learning more about this feature on the collaboration special site.

Event Duration

From Friday, May 8, 2026 at 1:00 a.m. (PDT) / 8:00 (GMT) / 9:00 (BST) / 18:00 (AEST) to Monday, May 25, 2026 at 7:59 a.m. (PDT) / 14:59 (GMT) / 15:59 (BST) / Tuesday, 26 May at 00:59 (AEST)

How to Participate

Begin the quest "A Journey to Remember" by speaking to the Remumu in Limsa Lominsa - Upper Decks (X:11.6 Y:11.2).

Rewards

Visit the special site for more details.
